speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe some of the factual details...Well first I wanted to ask you how you happened to come to Potlatch.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, I got sick of the East, mad or something. I spent all my money for a railroad ticket, landed in Leeds, North Dakota. Then I took a harvest, went into harvest there, followed the harvest up into Canada. And I had a friend in Potlatch...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ...That had come out, earlier.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. So I just came out to see him about in 1914. You couldn't get much work anywhere. So I put in six months out in the country milking cows for my board. And then I went into the Potlatch mill working nights tailing the edger. Well I belonged to the Masons of course, 50 year Mason, I don't have to pay any dues. And the boss of the townsite was a Mason. But prior to that I painted I think and papered every house in Potlatch.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: There wasn't quite as many then as there is now but...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And there was these four room houses, you had to paper one a day (chuckles). And I had to smell the green paper, of course, used arsenic...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Green oatmeal.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: ...to color it, and I got arsenic poisoning. So doc, old doc he put me up at Loon Lake up there in Washington for six months to recuperate. I imagine that my appendix broke because I never had any trouble after that (chuckles). And I guess the poisons from my appendix offset the poisons from the arsenic poisoning. So when we got back from there, well I worked at this or that. I was an invoice clerk in the Potlatch store—I don't know how long, do you? A year or two.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I'd say longer than that.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And during the war I was head mechanic in the garage. Come pret'near dying there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How's that?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh, it was in the basement. It was cold, 18 below zero I think. Started some Ford tractors a-goin1, and for some reason or another I stepped outside, and I knew right away that I had got too much carbon monoxide, because I was so dizzy I couldn't stand up. Probably if I'd a stayed in there another half hour, I wouldn't a been here now. So after that I got on the townsite, they was building nine houses up on what they call...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Nob Hill.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Nob Hill. And I put in the furnaces, hot air furnaces. And at the same time I'd get all the material—that is, the boss told me what he wanted and I'd go down the mill and get the order and get it up there to build these nine houses. Kind of straw boss I guess you'd call it. And when they made out the drafts, the lists of the draftees, I was...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: For the war.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. I was good on a typewriter, of course, and shorthand both. I graduated from the Albany Business College. And so when I got done, I had everything written up, the numbers and everything, and they thought that was great. Well how I come to get into the sheiff's office for some reason or another they'd called for a deputy from the north end of the county. And you know, I didn't know the sheriff, but the same time I got the job.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Hadn't Morgeridge died? And the deputy died.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I guess so. Something happened anyway. So I got the job and then of course being pretty good on the typewriter and pretty good with figures; why I was made undersheriff right away. And then I hadn't been there too long, because I did most of the outside work. God my wife and I travelled a million miles looking for different (chuckles) people. And after I got to be sheriff, why I made the first thing to do is to get acquainted with every sheriff in Oregon, Washington, Montana and Utah. And that paid off pretty big. 'Cause I wanted anything y'know, I could 6all 'em up. And they'd get right at it: "Oh yes, Hap, we'll get right at it." Well you know, if I hadn't made acquaintances with them they'd've said, "Yeah, I'll attend to it," but then they'd forget it, y'know. And then of course I worked with the FBI all the time. Yes, I used to go with the FBI agents from here all over the country, because I knew the country well. If they'd send an FBI agent here they always gave him orders to come see me the first thing (chuckles), 'because I could tell him where to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get acquainted with all these sheriffs now?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I used to just get in the car and go call on each one of em.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dust visiting?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well we generally had something to, oh kind of look into, and we stopped.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But I knew most of the chiefs of police around, y'know. (Chuckles.) Over in Portland once in that park down in front of the—what hotel was that?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I don't remember, but...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: 24 hours anyway in a one hour parking zone. 'Course we got a ticket. I went up to see the chief of police, name was Miles. It was only a dollar any way I guess, cheap way to park in those days, y'know. He said,"you know, you coulda gone back to Idaho, I wouldn't a been after ya." (Chuckles.) Well I visited a lot of different sheriffs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to ask you about some of the different stories and about some of the illegal activities that were going on. And one is the moonshining, which I know when you got in was quite a big...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: That was the main thing in those days. Yep. I was the undercover guy for the federals.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Always when he'd start wearing that beard, letting his beard grow and getting on his old malones, they knew something was gonna happen.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: There was going to be a raid somewhere.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah...They'd go out. They'd stay, sometimes two weeks they'd be out.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, sometimes we'd set on the still, y'know, night and day for a week or two, if it was a good size still. I don't know, I got a picture I guess of a still, if I can find it. (Break.)...by the name or Zimmerman. The sheriff there at Whitman County, they couldn't get him, the federals couldn't get him. They wanted me to see if I couldn't catch him. Gordon and I went over—that's the big fella. And I bought a gallon of moonshine, paid him twelve dollars and a half for it. And I asked him, I says, "What the hell, you got about 800 acres here, the best Wheatland in the country. What's the rason that you're fooling with this booze?" "Well," he says, "I'll tell ya. I had a friend in Spokane. He got fined 200. And I paid the 200 dollar fine and took 200 gallons of moon in pay." And he was selling that for twelve dollars and a half, it cost him a dollar a gallon—just couldn't stand it. Well when he come to trail he brought in a Spokane register book, showed where he had signed that book on the night that I was supposed to have bought the booze. And just as luck would have it, y'know that little half line at the bottom on most of those old ledgers? And he'd signed on that half line, that was the only place on the ledger that any- body had ever signed on that half line (laughs). So that cooked him right there. Well he fought it clear to the Supreme Court, and the government finally broke him...I put in a month or two in Spokane for the sheriff there, but that was looking for burglars, robbers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that when they robbed Groh out there at Bovill?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: That wasn't the time, that was a little bit later on. When they'd robbed Groh, they got 1500 worth of clothing there in Bovill, and I got a tip that there was a gang in Peaceful Valley with an Oregon license car, theywere supposed to have. And my wife and I went up there, and I guess we put in about two weeks.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I think so.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We'd go in there every day, couldn't find it. There was a long row of steps goes from the street up above down into that valley. Soooo we went up, stood up at the top of that one day, and I says, "Well, I guess we'll give it up, go home." And I finally decided to go back once more, so we went down in there, and by god spotted the car.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The meantime he'd got his teeth pulled, (Hap laughs) and then had to go out after he'd had his teeth pulled, with the police.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: The police, they knew I was there looking for it, and they was ready at a minute's notice. So when I spotted this car and the house where they're at, why of course I just called the police and made the raid. Got seven of the worst burgulars in the Northwest (chuckles). We got radios and everything else from all over that they'd stolen, y'know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they hard to arrest?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: They took them by surprise.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Police done the arresting, I didn't have anything to do with that. But the beauty of it was when they brought them in to the courtroom, I guess it was. Old John Groh, he was an old Dutchman, (chuckles) they brought 'em in, he jumped up there, "Ann, the sonofabitch, he got my pants on!" (Laughs.) But the reason that they raided that, there was one cap, just one cap that we could find that had Oohn Groh's name in it. If it hadn't been for that I don't know whether we could ever get a search warrant or not. But that was where I made my first. I was famous from then on. (Chuckles.)

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get to the cap? Did you go and look at their goods?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: That was after they'd arrested them.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: After they'd arrested them. Of course we searched, took all their stuff, y'know, radios and everything else that they had in the house there. But old 3ohn Groh, that old Dutchman, when the fella come in, had his pants on. He was going to fight with him right there (laughs). (Break.)...Where it turns down the Clearwater River before you get into Lewiston, y'know, the road down the Palouse. There used to be a gang down there, they called them Chickenhouse, wasn't it? Anyway there was about four of 'em, and they'd been mooning. The federals, they couldn't get 'em. Soooo of course they called for me, and I went down one rainy night. And I was representing myself as a—(chuckles) I was a good liar—moonshiner from Union Flats. And I said I had about 40 gallons of moon and I'd like to get rid of it, and I knew they could use it. Well they was interested right away. So I was to deliver the moon the next two three days. in the meantime I bought a lot of containers, and the last thing I bought was a pint of moonshine—I had to have a little to go home on, and I would replace it when I brought the other moon. And they had some that they'd got coal oil in, and I said, "Oh," I says, "I'll take that home and rerun it, not cost you anything." The federals was waiting outside, stationed there, y'know. (Chuckles.) Another thing y'know was to get the marked money, I gave them marked money for the moon that I'd bought, you see. So the federals went in and raided them. And it was about eight months before they come to trail. Y'know the government was pretty rough, they'd hardly ever give em less than eight months and a thousand dollar fine in those days. Any way when there come the trial time in the fall, Gerry Gelwick was running the soft drink joint, I don't know whether you...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Popcorn stand.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Whether you remember Jerry Gelwick or not?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard of him.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: You've heard of him, yeah.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He just died here not very long ago.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Anyway, there was one of these guys that was supposed to know every officer in the Northwest, the reason they couldn't get 'em. So he come into town just before the trial. He asked Gerry, he says, "What kind of looking guy is that man Moody anyway?" Well Gerry described me. "Well," he said, "that's the sonofabitch!" and then went in and plead guilty. He got eight months and a thousand dollar fine (chuckles). It was just something like that all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come the feds would have more trouble than you would to go in and break these guys?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Hap was little and un—you know.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I didn't look like an officer.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: No.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And of course I used to get throwed in jail, got (chuckles) throwed in once down in Lewiston, I was running around two three o'clock in the morning down there.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Course this was done on purpose.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: This wasn't.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh wasn't it? (Hap and Sam laugh.) Ok. All right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This one just happened to you. What happened?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Then when I got in there of course the chief knew me. But up in Spokane, why the detectives there1d pick me up, kick me around, throw me in jail, some pretense or another...We had three guys wasn't it—sold some mortgaged cattle or something here, and by god they couldn't get head nor tail. They knew they went over to the coast. So their wives lived in Lewiston. So I went down there one night to see 'em. I says, "You know, it's really none of my business, but I've been in Latah County jail for quite awhile,:;and I heard them talking about your husband." I says, "Be sure and don't write to 'em or try and call 'em up because," I says, "everything's covered, they're waiting." And then I fooled around awhile. "Well," I says, "I guess I'll go over towards the coast." "Well," they says, "you tell em!" (Laughs.) Oh, god. Gust that easy. I got home, the sheriff and I started for the coast, to the address that these women gave me. We got 'em, all three of 'em. Brought them back (chuckles). First time the women come up to visit 'em they saw me. They were pretty sore for a little bit, but they kind of got over it (chuckles). They thought it was a pretty clever trick. And then they got to see their husbands too, while they were in jail. But I was pretty lucky. After that Peaceful Valley trip I kind of made it...to go back once more. I went back once more and got all those robbers, you see. Well, Deobald at Kendrick during the war lost 11500 worth of tools that was stolen. And there was a kid saw a guy with a gun in his bedroll. So I hired the kid to stay with me.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: That kid...the guy told the kid to leave his bedroll alone.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: The kid got curious and found a gun in it I guess, or something. So we put in two weeks up there I believe, didn't we?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well, a long time anyway. (Hapchuckles.)

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whereabouts was that?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Orofino.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He went with you looking for the guy?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I kept the boy on guard to watch for him. Then I had the state patrol alerted to be sure and...why I had the description of the car or something, didn't I?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Pickup, I think.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Something anyway. So the kid saw him leave or something one day and he called me, and I got the state patrol out, and they stopped the guy in his car. And they didn't get the tools but he had a load of stolen tires (chuckles). And he was staying with an old bachelor up between Orofino and Kamiah in the brush back there, he had a shack of some kind. And I found there that they had some beaver hides, which was against the law. So I got the gamewarden, federals or something to go up there and search that. And they got them with these beaver hides. And I don't know how we come to...did we go out in the brush and search, or did they tell us or something?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I don't remember.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But anyway we got all that stolen tools from Kendrick. This old bachelor had hid 'em out there in the brush. And that was all there was to that (chuckles). Deobald gave me 20. Couldn't get tools for love nor money during the war, y'know. Hell, he was just simply out of luck, he had nothing to do with it. An officer ain't suppose to take money or rewards, he's supposed to work for nothing (chuckles). Deobald was bound I'd take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was bound you were going to take it you say?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The 20, uh-huh.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeh, Deobald was.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: After they got that guy, then some boys had reported seeing a body floating down the river, remember? So Hap went up with the officers to see if they couldlocate it. I drove along the bank in the car and they was in a boat. They never did find the body anyway. But it was so beastly hot. And we had water- mellon at noon, and Hap was so sick, I tell ya. And we was ready to come home with this Whitey, they called him, I don't know what his other name was, that had stolen the stuff. We stayed all night anyway before we started home. And this Whitey had stomach trouble. I don't know how long he was in jail up here. You know, you get to like all these guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do ya?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Course the main bootleggers that grew up around Pierce and Orofino were Kentuckians and North Carolinans, you know. Well the federals decided to raid them once, and soguess there was about four federals and the sheriff from here, I don't know how many others there was. And they decided to raid Pierce. So they sent me along ahead to buy. And you know them bootlegers, some of 'em I think was waiting for me. They wanted to get in jail and get something to eat, I guess. A lot of 'em, you know just...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Be a good place to spend the winter.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: The upshot was we brought down two busloads to the Hotel Helgerson there at Orofino, and they had the U. S. Commissioner right there to arraign 'em. You see we left word with the bus driver there at Orofino—what was his name— that he was to break down on the way in, and we would go by him with a busload of officers. And 'd sent word that there was a busload of company officals coning, Potlatch officals. So we got in ahead of the regular buss—he pretended to break down and let us by. Well, we had men enough so we had about every place covered inside of ten minutes. And I think we brought 18 down to and have them arraigned, a busload there, at Orofino. And I'd go ahead and buy and the federals would follow along. Well the first busload that they brought down, I was in the back seat of a car with the officers, you know. The kids in Orofino were on the sidewalk there, they'd wander around there and then look at me, you know. "Bet that old guy has made a lot of moonshine!" (Laughs.) Oh that was quite a lot of fun. But, it was hard work. We used sit on a still maybe three days and nights, waiting for 'em to come in. It's hard work. Old Gim Rice was the chief federal officer, he was stationed in Lewiston.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what was it like out there on the eastern part of the county, like around Bovill and all through the logging part of the country? I get a lot of stories about moonshining from some of the old guys that were young then, in the 20 s, let's say, before it was a federal crime, you know, when it was local option. It seems like a lot of guys were doing it in those days.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah, a lot of them you bet. Well they'd get pretty good money, you know. Easy way to make a living. You got to have water to run your stills. That was one drawback, you had to stay on the stream. Although we had one old guy packed water way back up on a dry knoll—old East, Gohn East, over there at Princeton. And we had one old guy there at Princeton, he dug a hole in the ground, put it down in there. Well, the sheriff and I got down in that hole before he got there. And the old fella come in—and grabbed him of course. 84 years old, I believe. And by god the federals wouldn't take him in. They said he was too old, they wouldn't take him in. He thought a panther had him when we jumped him down in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how would you decide who to go after? If a guy was smart, would he get away altogether if he kept it pretty much to himself?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, yes he would.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Somebody'd generally squeal.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well yeah, and of course they had to have somebody to sell it for 'em. We was over to Cove one night—let's see, that was some of your relation wasn't it?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh I wouldn't be surprised.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: (Laughs.) He had 40 gallons of moon hid in the haymow. I don't know how we found it out, some way. So we went over there, dug it out, and we pouredthat moonshine into the hog trough, and doggone pigs got drunker than the devil on it, y'know, and then squeeealed, and sang around there, squealing (chuckling). Oh gosh. He didn't make that, he just was a-hidin' it for old August Liesener down at Palouse. Those smart ones, they got somebody else to get caught. They didn't get caught themselves (laughs).

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everytime you found out about it, everytime it reached your attention, would you feel duty-bound to go and pursue it and get the guys?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah. 'Course as I say, I worked with the federals so much, and when they got balled up, why they'd send for me—well the other sheriffs too. Be because they knew that I could buy when there wasn't anybody else could, y'know, 'because I didn't look like an officer. It was funny the way some of 'em would act, y'know. Sometimes I'd buy a gallon, maybe forty gallons or something, and I'd show 'em my badge, and some of them would look at it, y'know, and look at me, and they'd drop it like a hot potato (laughs). Yeah, but you know a bootlegger never had it in for an officer, hardly ever. They had it in for the stoolpigeon. 'Course there were stoolpigeons everywhere buying, y'know. But you couldn't put 'em on the stand, a grand jury wouldn't believe »em, but they would believe an officer. And the bootleggers never..."Well," they said, "that's your business, you're supposed to do that." They never...Oh once in a while one would want to shoot you if he got a chance. But you didn't have to worry about them, I knew if they was going to they wouldn't say they were going to (chuckles). They wouldn't warn me in advance. But the boot leggers didn't have it in for the officer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well who were these stoolpigeons then? Did these guys get paid for making an arrest?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The stool pigeons couldn't make an arrest.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But they would buy and tell. But they couldn't make an arrest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they got paid off if they set up an arrest.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yes, yes.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They got paid off. They was called informers by the government (laughs), but they was called stoolpigeons by the bootleggers. And they'd get up with these dances, y'know. See there was a dance hall damn near on every...There's one on American Ridge, one at Deary, y'know...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Thorn Creek.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And at Bovill, and over at Kennedy Ford, and out here at Rowland's Park... And we had to police those dances all the time, y'know. Well it was kind of fun, y'know, we'd be driving in, and see some of 'em there with a flashlight, y'know, looling in the brush, and we knew of course they was looking for a cache. So one of the officers would get out with a flashlight, go alongside with 'em y'know, and help 'em look. All at once they'd look up and see who it was: "Gesus Christ, the law!" and away they'd go. We'd keep hunting till we found their cache (chuckles).

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have much trouble with drunks? Were you supposed to arrest a guy who was acting drunk at a dance? How did that work?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, sometimes I'd have a little trouble. Gordon's about six foot six, weighed about 260. And he'd just take an arm under their neck, lift 'em up off the ground and walk off with 'em. I left the arresting to the big guys, I didn't do much of it. Yeah, I wish Gordan could talk, he could tell ya. He loves to tell stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I'm gonna get in touch with him if he's well enough to speak to me. Was it against the law for a guy to be drunk, to be drinking? Was that illegal?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yes, it was illegal to have liquor in a bottle or anything, you know. Possession, you could get hooked for that. That's where we got 'em at the dances. Get drunk, or catch 'em drinking, as a rule. Take their bottle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If a guy was drunk and he was pretty quiet, he wouldn't stand much a chance of getting arrest, I'd imagine?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no. If he kept quiet. Went out when Ing Aas was my deputy—he lives in Clarkston. And he was raised out on this Burnt Ridge, out east of Troy there. I sent him in one night into the dance hall, he hadn't been on very long. He come out and he says, "My God," he says, "they're all drunker than hell in there." "Well," I says, "you go back in and I'll drop in in a few minutes." And old Ing never got over it—he said when I stepped through the door you could hear a pin drop, there wasn't any of 'em drunk (laughs). He couldn't understand that. That was a fact, though. There was of a lot of them be drinking that went carousing, but let an officer show up, they wasn't drunk...Another funny thing happened here in town. Ing hadn't been on only about—I don't know whether that was his first night as jailer or not. A fella by the name of Bill Hill had an apartment down there, called up. He says, "I got a peeping torn down here, I wish you'd come down and get him." "Well," Ing says, "I'm alone, and I can't very well get away." He says, "Dust get the chief of police." "Well that's the sonofabitch doing the peeking"—and it was! (Laughs.) The chief of police was trying to catch the old guy...Oh gosh, we've had some funny things happen. When we first started in down at Kendrick, we got an Indian to buy for us, because we knew that he could buy. So we gave him some money to go and buy a bottle of booze. We lost track of him—in fact we didn't try and watch him. But we found him after awhile. He was drunker than hell. "Me buy! Me buy!" But he'd drank up all the evidence, we couldn't do a thing. So after that we followed the Indian, we kept him in sight (chuckles)."Me buy! Me buy!" He drank up all the evidence. We had a district judge here, old Gudge Steel—quite a hand to drink himself, but he would back up the officers. And we had a Lawyer by the name of Keane, and he was defending all the bootleggers. in court one day, and I was on the stand, and they had a bottle about two-thirds full. And Keane says, "How do you know that that is moonshine whiskey?" He was trying to get in that I'd drank some of it, y'see. Old judge, he rared up, he said, "If Mr. Moody says that's moonshine whiskey, it's moonshine whiskey. Proceed with the case (laughs). The old Gudge would backed me up. He scared the big deputy. We had open court, y'know, and I'd send him over. I don't know what happened, he'd get tongue-tied and stammered. The judge'd send him back, and tell him to send me over. What was his name, big guy, y'know?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Garrison?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Garrison. The old judge would call him over, on purpose, y'know. He just couldn't hold with court for some reason.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about a couple of these real colorful lawmen who were here in the early days? I know you knew Pat Malone. H Ms (Chuckles.) Yeah, he was quite a fixture. We had him with us one night out at Rowland's Park. We'd picked up six or eight drunks and lined 'em up on the running board—in those days there was a running board on the car. Well a fight started over towards the dance hall, and old Pat, he couldn't stand it any longer, he had to run over there. When he got back, the drunks'd all left of course, run off. Summerfield (chuckles) said, "What the hell ya doing here Pat, letting all these get away?" Old Pat he stumbled around, "Oh well, hell, we got enough anyway." (Laughs.) We were going down to Genesee, and started after a moonshiner—never mind that, he was just a bootlegger. I took after him, and it was darker thanthan the devil. I come to a ditch about six foot deep and into it I went, and I couldn't get out of it. Old Pat was shooting away at the guy he thought, and Gordan, he says, "Heeey Pat," he says, "that's Hap, don't shoot at him!" (Laughs.) Oh it was really quite a lot of fun, a lot of hard work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I heard that he had a reputation for not being too tough, Pat did, if he could avoid it, he'd just as soon...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh no, he was good for that Bovill bunch. Oh they'd mind him, all right, if he got after 'em. He didn't bother 'em much. But he was a good man for that locality, because they respected him all right, would mind him, but he wouldn't throw 'em in, unless it got too bad. He was a powerful devil. He got a grip on ya, you wouldn't get away. They told a story back when he was in Detroit, they put him on one of the toughest districts there for some reason or another. Anyway there was some tough guys. One morning he come in wheeling a couple of 'em in a wheelbarrow, says, "Here's your tough guys!" (Laughs.) Right in court. I've got an idea that's true, that's the story they tell of him. But he wasn't afraid of anything, I'll give him credit for that. He wasn't afraid of the devil himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's got something of a reputation for being a drinker himself out there,

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He did drink, um-hm.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: He drank himself. He was a great man to cuss.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Every other word.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They had some in court, come IWW's. We had 200 of 'em in a stockade down there years ago, barbwire. And when they come to trial they had some lawyers from Montana, some of the best lawyers in the country, y'know. The IWW, they had money. Well they put Old Pat on the stand, and one of these high priced lawyers, he says, "Isn't it a fact, Mr. Malone, that you're prejudiced against these poor workingmen?" Old Pat, he rared up. "I'm prejudiced against any sonofabitch that puts the red flag above the stars-and-stripes!" (Laughs.) By god they convicted them. I think that done more to convict them than anything else. But he did have—the judge would say, "Now Mr. Malone, don't, don't use so much profone language, just, just tell this thing as it is." "Well Jesus Christ, I'm trying to!" (Laughs.)

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I don't think he realized that he was...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I don't think he realized he was swearing.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ...swearing, you know, he just did this...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It just came so natural to him.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Gust rolled out of him. And he was witty. Up to Bovill once, they brought a prostitute in from St. Maries on a stretcher. And she was laying there on the stretcher on the platform there at the Bovill station. Old Pat comes stomp ing along. She looked up at him. "Hellooo, Pat, you've slept with me many a time, didn't ya?" And Old Pat, he went stomping by, "Poor lady, she's out of her head!" (Laughs.) Oh yeah. Pat weighed close to 300 pounds, y'know. He was stout. That reminds me of a federal officer, Bulldog Gohnson, they called him. He used to shoe 40 horses, 40 head of horses a day back in Minnesota. He got on the sheriff's office up there at Coeur d'Alene. And he came dragging a couple of tough guys in. The sheriff says, "Well what the heck? What are you doing here? These fellas are unconscious." "Oh I just yumped their heads together!" He wasa powerful guy, goshdarn he was stout. He could yump their heads together too, believe me. And he was on the federal, he was always on these raids. Him and a fella by the name of Sam Webb, they was stationed here up at Coeur d'Alene, and they was in on all these raids that I went ahead to buy.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Then there was another one that had been a preacher, that was an FBI, thatworked with those two.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. They was driving through South Idaho one day. This Bulldog Gohnson was driving, he chewed snoose. Every little while he'd open the window and spit out and the'd swerve the car. They drove about 70 miles an hour. Old Sam Webb couldn't stand it much longer. And he had a high squeaky voice y'know, and he weighed about 300 pounds. Well he says, "Spit on the window you sonofabitch, I'll wipe it off!" (Laughs.) Oh gosh. That sounded just like Sam Webb too. I'll bet he did tell him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to crack down much on prosititutes? Was that part of the job?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh, we wasn't bothered much in this county.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: That was before you.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, well it was earlier days. That's before we ran the office. There wasn't many prostitutes around. You know, there was that up here at Princeton, you know where Princeton is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah we had—what was it? 26 saloons there in the old days.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: 17, wasn't it?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: 17 saloons in those days?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I can't remember 'em but...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They had prostitutes then. I can't hardly feature Princeton having 17 saloons.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: 17 buildings, hardly.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But that was in the logging days, when they was logging. You know at one time the line between the prairie and the woods went right through the edge of Moscow here. West was all prairie, wheat land—east was all big trees. Probably you can remember the big stumps maybe yet. Up around Troy and in through there, used to be stumps there 10 feet high. Used to have a lot of snow in those days (chuckles). Then that was the line, and then therewas an Indian trail from the the Coeur d'Alenes down to the Nez Perces right along the edge of this forest. It was allll dense forest east and all prairie west. Her father came here in 79. He was looking for wheat land.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He had to log. He logged off our place.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yes. Ranches those days. All these farmers logged off their own places, a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard there was pretty wild times there back in Palouse City and up on the Palouse back right around the turn of the century.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Her father used to drive logs in this logging country into Palouse and their sawmill, into Colfax. Her father drove the logs every spring on the log drive.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh I really don't remember when they were so tough, but then I guess. Maybe they were. H Ms You know a guy wanted to sell the site of Spokane Falls now.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Trade.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Trade for a saddle pony. He wouldn't trade. A saddle pony was worth more than Spokane Falls was. There was nothing there but rocks and bull pine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wanted to trade with your dad?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: y dad, when my dad first came out here. 'Course maybe that guy didn't even own the land. Who knows? (Laughs.)

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, he came here in '79.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I want to ask you a little more about the IWW's. What was it that they did that broke the law, that required their being arrested?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, they was striking for one thing, and I believe that they...Let me see, what was it they was picked up for?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well they didn't want the other guys to come in and work either, did they? Isn't that the reason that you was...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They wouldn't let the loggers work. I stayed one month, a little better, on the dividing line between the outside and the loggers over on Bear Creek... 21

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Was it Three Bear?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Three Bear, over in there. And I had more trouble with the loggers, lumber jacks, than I did with the strikers. 'Cause of course I kept 'em, I never would let em go over the line, but the damn loggers, whn they'd get drunk on the Saturday night, they'd go out and want to clean up on the strikers, you see. So I was between the devil and the deep blue sea most of the time (chuckles). But I worried over there on the Three Bear though, for fear that the strikers would start a fire or something. My god there was no way to get out of there, only one road. It'd've been pretty bad. And these IWW's, they got the abbreviation that "we won't work..."

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: "I won't work."

speaker: Hap Moody
words: "I won't work," yeah. That was their motto. But apparently they would try to...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Wouldn't they be almost same as the strikers now?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, might be, I don't remember now just...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Course they belong.to a union too.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But it was something against the law anyway, because they tried them in court and they convicted them.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: When Hap first come to work, the jail was down under the old treasurer's office actually, wasn't it?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yep. All it was was just a hole in the ground.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The sheriff's office was on the main floor at the old courthouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Charlie Summerfield like? I heard he was rather tougher on the loggers than Pat was.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah. Charlie was tough. He was a champion wrestler. He was stout, and he really enforced the law. And he'd drunk himself occasionally (chuckles).

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Not very often.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Not very often. But he got paresis of the brain finally. Doc said that he was dangerous, and he finally committed suicide. He got scared, afraid he'd kill somebody. God, Gordan was afraid of him. Ing never was. Well I never was. 'Course he'd have these blackouts, and when he came to he'd make me promise to stay with him. And I did, I stayed with him. The doctor said he was dangerous. He knew he had paresis of the brain. That's one reason he committed suicide.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Is that about the same as cancer? Is that a growth, what is it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know, I haven't heard of it before.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I don't know, it's a brain diease.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a very serious guy, or was he pretty funny?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He was serious most of the time. He could kid, but then he took his job, it was a serious business.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he used to talk very much about these old times back around World War I when they had this IWW trouble and that kind of thing? And back when the old moonshining...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah, he'd talk about it. 'Course he was in World War I himself.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He had a: bullet in his back.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: A bullet up next to the spine. They never dug it out. He still had it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about gambling? That was against the law too, was it?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, oh yes, gambling was against the law, selling cigerattes to minors was against the law (chuckles).

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cause I know the loggers in the camps, they gambled.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. Well it didn't bother us very much, just in town.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Unless they got in a rumpus.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, there was quite a bit of gambling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you pack a gun yourself very much?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Very seldom. 23

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: When you went out on trips though, you did.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Gust here in town, he didn't.

Gust if I was called out on the road to stop a car or anything like that, I took a sawed-of shotgun (chuckles). They'll stop when they look into a sawed-off shotgun, y'know. I trained Gordan to...He always packed a pistol. I told him if he ever had to draw it on anybody to be sure and tremble, shake, as if...(laughs.) He would tremble holding that gun y'know, and boy, they'd go up into the air. But I don't know, I never packed a gun much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have to use one?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Never did. Well there was a kid put in reform school when he was nine years old. And he was in corrigable. Gudge Nelson used to keep him in his office during the day to catch him stealing. By god he never caught him, but I'd have to go down and get everything out under his mattress the next morning (chuckles). He'd steal from the judge. But anyway we got a call, Gordan and I, that a fella had stolen a.45, I believe it was revolver and some ammunition down at the second had store...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: This was after he'd got bigger.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: ...On Main Street. So Gordan and I jumped in the car and went down past Main and went around and saw the kid coming down the other way. Well I jumped out of the car, took after him. He started to run. And he reached around, and he had this.45 hung on his belt. Well (chuckles), I thought maybe we was going to have a battle so I got my revolver out. But what he was trying to do was to unfasten it and he let it drop. Well, that's the nearest I ever come to shooting anybody, because I was just about ready to crack down on him. Well he kept a-runnin', and I shot a couple times in the air over his head and he stopped. It was this kid that I'd put in a reform school when he was nine years old, but he was 19 or 20 see, and six foot tall. I didn't recognize him at all, until I got up to him and asked him his name. Well, the kid got put on probation, and got him into the army some way, and he got to be an expert rifleman, and made good. Carrico was the name, Ivan Carrico.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever very scared, I mean when you were going into a still or into a situation?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I never was afraid of anybody much but a crazy man. I was always afraid of a crazy guy (chuckles).

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Tell him about the lady that was crazy out here.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, an old lady got crazy, and Gordan and I went out to see if we could get her...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Wasn't Charlie out there too?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No. Gust Gordan and I.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Wasn't he? Okay.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And I went to the window, and she had this revolver, and I'd heard.-she wanted to get a.22 rifle. So I went out with the idea of trading her a.22 rifle for this revolver, see.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: So's he could get here, because she had been reported as insane.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Insane, y'know. And I kept a-talkin' to her through this window, trying to get her to open the window. She kept that gun on me all the time, y'know. Oh I says, "This gun ain't loaded, of course." "Oh yes it is!" And she showed me it was (chuckles).

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: She had it pointed right at his stomach, so Gordan said.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Pointed right at my stomach all the time. So I decided that wasn't going to work, so we throwed a gas bomb through the window, and the damn thing didn't go off. So (chuckles) she came out of there with her apron, walked out to the edge of the creek there. And Gordan's laying right there. 'Course Gordan grabbed her right there. Took her to the asylum...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They kept it from you, they didn't tell you what they were doing?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I knew that they was working up there or something, but I had no idea what kind of place or what kind of a layout it was. And then years afterwards, well, "This is where we did so-and-so," and...(Hap chuckling.) I would have been a nervous breakdown, that's all there is to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe that's why he didn't tell ya.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Course now a lot of the things Charlie used to say, "Now don't even tell your wife!"

speaker: Hap Moody
words: (Laughs.) And so we didn'ttell our wives. She rode with me thousands of miles. I took her with me all the time when I was going through the different states.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: We hunted for...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Came hunting for somebody, you know.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ...Kids, women, men. And when this non-support law was in so much, I tell ya, we used to go over on the coast for people, for somebody. We'd get 'em and bring 'em home, you know. Maybe they'd keep 'em a day or two and out they'd go. 'Course actually they couldn't pay if they was in jail anyway. There was something sadly wrong with the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Something sad?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Sadly wrong with the setup that...I know there's a lot of men won't pay alimony and help with the kids and this and that. But they don't, and you get and go a way off and get 'em and bring them to jail, maybe they'd keep 'em a little while and turn 'em loose. To me it was a big expense. They can't pay while they're in jail. But I suppose they have to do something or maybe they'd all have done that. Now since Womans' Lib I guess, I hear they're going to turn it the other way around (chuckles). Women are going to have to pay alimony.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that'll probably take awhile.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah! Um-hm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think it was that made guys break the law? You seem to think that a lot of the guys that did something wrong were really basically pretty good people.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I think they all have some good point to them. I remember one guy that they had over there at the jail, he cooked for them. And there was a kid in there that they was going to have to take to Saint Anthony's, and that guy used to tell him—I'd hear him tell 'em the things they shouldn't do, and this and that, y'know. Well, to me he was helping the kids. But I think they all... We never did take a kid down to St. Anthony but what I could have brought him back home.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: (Laughs.) Yeah, they was always so polite y'know, they'd open the car door and wait on her hand and foot. She used to feel pretty bad, have to take 'em to St. Anthony. I always took her with me when I took 'em down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell her a lot of the things that you did when you were doing them? Was it just certain things that you kept back? (Hap chuckles.) I mean, you must have told her a lot of the things that happened.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah, she was with me a lot of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She couldn't help but know.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But like sitting up there on those stills for so long and...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I didn't tell her about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would just sit there and wait for the guy that had the still to come?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. Sometimes two, three days and nights. Yeah, up around Clarkia, in there, there was some pretty big stills in that country. Plenty of water there, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were all the stills built on pretty much the same kind of style, or was there a lot of different ways?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no, no. They all had to have the barrels for mash, you know. And then they had to have water to cool off the coils, so when they run the mash through this still, y'know. She was made out of anything. We got one still made out of galvenized can. What do they call it—those copper wires, there's supposed to be copper in the still.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Otherwise it can be poison.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Be poisonous, y'know. Some of it was (chuckles) poisonous, and some of it was real good liquor. 'Course it was new. We had a bunch down in Genesee, they made liquor out of potatoes—and by god, that was about 200 proof (chuckles), Boy it was stout! It was stout.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the sheriff's office went easier on guys that were locals and here a long time than on people that were just, let's say...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, 'course I had friends that was bootleggers that kept me posted. And the sheriff at Newport, Washington, he would turn his back about five minutes and let a load of liquor go through, forty cases, for 200. He got 200 everytime a load went through. Yep, I knew a lot of other officers did the same thing. I was offered 200 a month to let slot machines run. 200 a month— I was only getting about 90 (chuckles). But I wouldn't take a chance.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The thing is—if a guy ever does this once, he's in for it.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, and you're bound to get caught sooner or later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean that they can use that against him in the future?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Sure, the next time they'd want him to do something a little bit bigger, and then it keeps getting bigger and bigger. That's where they make the mistake—the first time letting somebody bribe him. 'Course a lot of 'em get by with being bribed, I guess, so they say.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We had one prosecuting attorney that...We'd try to raid these, get these pinball machines, not attorneys. Pinball machines (chuckles).

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Slot machines.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Okay. 28

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And we had a prosecuting attorney, and everytime we tried to raid...I had a deputy up at Bovill that kept me posted on all these slot machines. But everytime we'd raid they wouldn't be there. So of course we knew that the prosecutor was tipping them off. So one day I went up to the probate judge, and I says, "You make it known that you're going over to the coast on a vacation. I want to get these slot machines." So he did, and I had the probate judge get the warrants out without letting anybody know it. Ordinarily, y'know, they went to the prosecuting attorney. I got 'em, I got the machines. And I was scared to death that the prosecutor would frame me (laughs).

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could that have happened?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh, he probably could have framed me some way or another. But he went to Hawaii, he's over there now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well when you say that you had moonshiner friends that kept you posted, you mean they kept you posted as to what moonshine was being made?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yeah. Competition. They'd turn in their (laughs) competition. Hell, I'd pay 'em for it. (Chuckles.) I was just as crooked as they was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well it seems like you never could have stopped it, doesn't it? I mean it'd just be impossible to stop.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no, you never could stop it.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And that's just kind of like telling a kid they can't do anything. Grownups are just like little kids. If they're not supposed to have it, they're gonna, they're gonna have it. Isn't that right?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, just couldn't stop it. It was against the laws. Guveniles to have beer, well hell, they get it in their own home. All they have to do is go to the refrigerator and get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, from what I hear, it sounds like the lumberjacks had a pretty tough life and a hard life, and they needed it, a lot of them. I mean it was important to be able to blow off steam somehow. 29

speaker: Hap Moody
words: A lumberjack would work all winter, get his pay, and go to Spokane, and in three days he'd be broke. Flatter than a pancake. That was a lumberjack for you. Yeah. Then he'd go back into the woods and get another stake, come out and spend it all in two three days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there ever much trouble with lumberjacks coming from the camps into the small towns around here and blowing off their steam?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No. Oh they'd come in and get drunk, get throwed in jail, some of 'em, but they really wasn't too bad

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any times that you can think of that Hap hasn't thought of, some of the incidents that you know about?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: (Chuckles.) I can't think of any right now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What it was like to be...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We had a man run a restaurant down here on Main Street. He run it, oh I guess for two three months. Then he beat it with all the money. He was from North Dakota, and there was 200 dollar reward out for him, a lot of money in those days. And I used to eat there, y'know, and I paid him, talked to him, y'know, and after he left I got a tip that he was in Spokane, so my wife and I went to Spokane, got in the hotel across from the hotel where he was supposed to be. Well the owner must've made a mistake. I got the chief deputy at Spokane, and warned him that I wanted him. And he said this landlady would tell him if the guy was there. Well she either didn't tell him or else she... He didn't get him anyway. Anyway he got out a day or two before I was ready to abandon, so I lost the 200 reward, and the guy both. I don't know were he went, back to Dakota, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he wanted for?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: For absconding with the restaurant money. He stayed right there at the cash register. I always paid him. Was he supposed to have bought the restaurant or something? I don't remember now. 30

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Don't remember.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Anyway he got away with quite a bunch of money...Oh, we had quite a time with bum checks, y'know, bum checks. Have to hunt 'em up and make 'em good. And of course that's bad yet.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I don't believe there's quite as much right now though...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ...As there was. Maybe we just don't hear about it.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: About the time the University boys was going home, why you had to watch out for bum checks.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ....And stolen cars.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, stolen cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of that going on here in the old days when they had cars?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, not any more than there is now. I remember the first stolen car I went after, a Ford—guy left it running down in front of Davids', come out it was gone. Well, they got it over at St. Regis, Montana. And talk about a cattle trail! (Laughs.) Gust a trail was all it was. A good thing there weren't many cars on the road in those days, there was no place to turn out. But we went ooer to St. Regis anyway, and brought it back. It was always my good luck to have to drive the stolen car back.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Did you tell him about the guys that broke out, crawled out the roof of the jail?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: (Laughs.) Oh we had a couple of prisoners...Was they federal prisoners?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I don't remember what they was in for.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But anyway they dug out through the roof. Happened to be a place where the steel didn't come together.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And the pipe went...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And y'know I could cover that hole with my hat, and it wasn't too big a hat either. They soaped themselves and got out through there and made a matteress rope and got out. And they got back to Wisconsin, was it?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You stopped in Wisconsin. That's where Henry's...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. The funny part of it was they went downtown and stold a Buick car that belonged to the government. Well they stored it there through the winter and nobody thought anything about it. If they saw it was gone, they probably thought the government man had come and picked it up. So they got clear back anyway to Wisconsin, and then they got picked up for something—I don't know, anyway, some minor infraction. And they called up, that's the first we knew that the damn car was stolen (chuckles).

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You was on the way back to get 'em, wasn't you, when you heard that the car was stolen?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I don't know, I can't remember. So we went back there, Ing and I—that's the chief deputy—we went back and got 'em. Well we got back into Montana, reached Frenchtown, stopped for gas. That doggone deputy of mine went down to the store to buy some bananas and left them two in the back seat. 'Course they shackled together. Well the doggone gas man, he filled the car, and then what did the damn fool do but go and put the key back in the lock. There they was there, and both of us gone. One of 'em said, "Let's take off." The other one said, "No, no, that damn Moody'd shoot us." (Laughs.) But I sure gave my deputy heck for leaving them guys there alone. 'Course it wasn't his fault. You thought the damn garageman would've give us the keys. But no, he goes and sticks it in the car.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: aybe he didn't know.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: (Chuckles.) Well he could see they was handcuffed together and they had leg irons too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they didn't take off, huh?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They didn't take off. But one of 'em wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They let you know. 32

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Let's see, somebody ran out of the jail one time when Ing was there. Now what was that?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Asked you to mail a letter?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: No this fella, they took off. (Hap chuckles.)

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of some of these other places in your travels as compared to Moscow and North Idaho? Did you find much difference in the different places that you went, and what the people were like?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, we got the best treatment down at Salt Lake. They had a man there to take visiting officers all through the tabernacle and out to the copper mines. A man did nothing else from the sheriff's office but entertain visiting sheriffs. That was the only place that we got as much attention, I guess.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well we generally didn't have that much time, anyway.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I can't remember what we went down for that time.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: I don't know, looking for somebody, I suppose.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He didn't take us through the tabernacle, am I right? There's something that.•.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. Took us through the tabernacle, but you can't get in the temple.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh, it's the temple, yeah, it's the temple you can't get into.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But they took us all through the tabernacle.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah, um-hm.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: You can enter that building, in five minutes it holds about 5,000. Probably you know about it. And you stand way at the other end, drop a pin, you can hear it drop.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: They did the same thing when my dad first came out. I can remember he used to tell about going in there and dropping a pin.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: You just whisper there, y'know. The acoustics'd beat anything on earth.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: We stayed over hoping to hear that big choir that we'd hear on the radio. 33

speaker: Hap Moody
words: But they didn't operate for us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you travelled together, you kept him company pretty much...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, I had a car...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: We used our own car.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We always used our own car. I drove a Packard in those days, it was my own car. This car we got now of course, the seats go back and you can make a bed in it. Oh I guess we'd had that fixed to, yeah, we had the Dodge fixed that way, didn't we?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: We had the Dodge and we had the Buick that was fixed up.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: When we was gone we'd drive into some sheriff's yard...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: ...Or state park.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Gust sleep right in the car.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Now we'd be afraid to.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And we used to stay at the state parks a lot too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that things have gotten much worse as far as trusting people and breaking the law, that sort of thing, than it used to be?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh I don't know, it seems that way. There's more of them, I guess, is all.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: It's easier to hear about these things today, I think—radio, TV.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, there's more publicity I think, or something, I don't know. It seems that there's a lot of criminals running around. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there ever any organized crime, what you would call big, bigger time crime?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Not here, not here, there wasn't population enough in those days. 'Course I say the sheriff gave me 200 to stay a month around Spokane skidrow listening for burglars, break-ins. I had to leave booze alone (laughs). I didn't fool with booze away from my own dunghill, 'because a lot of the officers was right in with the bootleggers, y'know. You didn't know what to do, I just watched for burgulars... (Break.)...in from Seattle and the coast, and they tried to shut the mill down, y'know. But the Potlatchers wanted to work, they didn't want to strike. And they came to me and asked me if I would see that they got down to the mill, passed through the strikers, striker's line, you know. I said, "Sure, if you want to work, I'll see that you get down to the mill." (Chuckles.) So I posted—well, I went myself, I had the deputy with me. I stood right there at the line. I told the strikers they could talk to 'em peacefully if they wanted to, but I said, "Don't you ever try to stop one of 'em. First day somebody throwed a rock at the first guy went through, and of course I nabbed him right away. That wound that up, and they didn't throw any more rocks. But they pretended to take pictures of everyone that went by, you know. Well I knew there was no film in the camera, it was just bluff. There was three different times I guess, wasn't it...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I think.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: ...That I had to go over.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But they never really caused any trouble.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no, they didn't dare to. The law was very plain—if they did any strike breaking, they had to do it peacefully, they couldn't use force. And I had some nephews that wanted to work, and they'd get the truck and go right down through (chuckles) the line. And I told them, I says, "If they want to run over you—if you want to get out in the road and they want to run over you, it's all right with me." So they stayed out of the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there local guys too who were not working, who were out on strike in sympathy with these other guys?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: ost of the strikers was brought in from outside, I suppose was paid by the striker's union or whatever it is. (Break.)

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: (Describing her first plane ride:)...On the flat west of Potlatch down near the junction. And it had two passengers. Virgil (Adair, her cousin) wasn't driving it. But then anyway I got in (chuckles), and the other guy got in too, I guess. Anyway the pilot came and he said, "You know, this fellow that's going up is supposed to turn over. Will you be afraid? (Chuckles.) I said, "No," but you know it felt just like—I was scared to death though— but it felt just like my head was down between my knees. in those days they charged 15 for, I don't know whether it was five minutes or—anyway, they'd take you around over Potlatch and around. 'Course I didn't have to pay for my ride, but that was my first airplane ride.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Now she's crazy to ride on 'em, she wants to ride in the jets. We go with the football team, you know, every year, chartered planes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Say, I want to ask you about that now. How did you start becoming Mr. Vandal? When did that begin?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: That started 40 years ago. I don't know.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: They didn't even have football around when he was a boy.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, I never saw football till I came West. I never saw a ski till I came West, y'know. Now back home is the greatest ski country in the world. Stow, Vermont? I run barefoot all over them mountains, there was no skis in those days.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You never started going to football games until after you started working for the sheriff's office, did ya? Or did you when you was in the garage over there?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, I don't think I did. See, after I got in the sheriff's office, it was my job to guard the line up there when they registered. I had to sit there and watch them. Then I had to go... 36

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You fellas patrolled the campus then.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, I had to patrol the campus. in those days the sheriff had to do that. So I don't know, I got to watching them, I guess.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And when we moved here, the lady that we rented from wouldn't have a foot ball player, they was too rough on things. And so after we bought the place, football players is who we rented to.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: See when Staley was coach, he bargained to rent to his players, you know.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: If they needed an apartment.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: He guaranteed the rent. And we had lots of football players I guess, off and on, for many years.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Then it just got so, well the kids here in the house—they had a friend that wanted to rent it, and well now we don't have any football players. Not that we have anything against football players, but you know, it's just one of those things.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Whoever leaves generally has somebody they want to put in there...

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Got a friend, or...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We don't got to look for renters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Of course in the old days there wasn’t any forward passing. That was, started, oh around in bout, in the thirties, I imagine. My first football was given to me by Leo Callan in ’34, and it was oval-shaped, you know, bigger, oval-shaped instead of pointed. I still have it, I guess, down in the basement somewhere. Well, then I get to traveling with the boys to all the games that were near. I didn’t have to pay and all.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He was almost a mascot for a while. Then I quit work and I spoiled that.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Now we have to pay. Bertha laughs But, well, one reason I think we went back to Kalamazoo, one of my deputies, he graduated and he lives in Chicago, and he was one unintelligible two men got away and got back to Wisconsin, other came back and he went on down to Texas or somewhere.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Wisconsin, his folks lived there. Grandpa.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And he brought the other car back, I let him take my car and we brought the stolen car back.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: unintelligible went to Minneapolis after that car, didn’t ya?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Went through Minneapolis…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh okay cross-talk

speaker: Hap Moody
words: unintelligible in Minneapolis. Wound up right in the center of Minneapolis and says “ unintelligible to Wisconsin” and he went and left us right in the middle Hap laughs. But he acted as jailer. I put him through school, in other words.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Helped him through school.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And he appreciated it, he started a scholarship in my name Hap laughs,

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Scholarship fund.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Back… I don’t know, I well, I used to like to watch ‘em practice for some reason, I never missed practice hardly at all. unintelligible And then I got to be what you’d call a mascot, I guess, and the coaches always liked it because I was there no matter how stormy and bad the weather was, I was always there and the boys thought if I could stand it, they could, and so… Hap laughs The coaches kind of looked at me to see if unintelligible bad weather. Sam: So it sounds like you really helped the team keep up their spirits.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. Course unintelligible alumni and I never went to school. Sam: Do you think that the teams have gotten much better through the years or was there a time when the teams were better than they are now?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh, I don’t know, it’s got to be quite a, quite a job to coach a team nowadays. I really think this man Troxel has pretty good luck, he’s pretty well liked by the kids. And he had pretty good luck in south Idaho. He’s got pretty good lines.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He’s a politician.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, he’s a politician, that’s what it takes. You know, to be a good coach, you got to be a good politician, too. We seen him at Johnny’s a while back, a couple weeks ago, and this Troxel had ten or fifteen boys in Johnny’s for lunch, and I don’t think any one of them could get out through these doors without bumping their heads. unintelligible trying to get ‘em to come to Idaho, well he talked them into coming, you know. He’s a good talker. The other fellow Robbins was a fine man, but he just, well he just didn’t have the gift of gab Hap laughs

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: We liked Robbins awfully good. I think he’s a nice guy.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. Sam: From what you’re saying, it sounds like the sport has gotten a lot bigger than it used to be. I mean there’s a lot more going in that you have to do, sounds like.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oh yes, it’s got, takes more money all the time, you know. Can’t get scholarships enough, you know, they get as many players as they want, school has to kind of sort out what they think is the best.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And I’m glad that Hap’s got that unintelligible interested since he retired because otherwise life might be kind of drab.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: She’s ready to go to all the games.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He’s, uh…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: First time, we went to Texas twice, first time was on a, four motor job, we had to change in Denver, took eight hours to get down there, and the last time I went down there, went down four hours on a jet. Same going back to Chicago, you know. Couple of hours, you’re there. And it goes unintelligible Johnson back and he took us all over Chicago. Down in Texas, we have a renter down there, he played with the Houston Oilers, named Jim Norton, been playing with the Oilers for years. Course he took care of us down there. When we go back to Kalamazoo, unintelligible Johnson, he’ll be there, and he knows where the plane’s going to land, it don’t land at O’Hare main field, it places outside, you know, he’d be right there with his car to pick us up, bring us back to the plane, step from his car into the plane. He really takes care of us. And they say he’s starting this scholarship too. I don’t know what that will come to, not very much I don’t think Hap laughs. cut

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Two pretty good players. Sam: So they really cracked some ribs, huh?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah. It wasn’t too bad, just tried to breathe for a while. And I wouldn’t even know it. unintelligible autographed by Walker and Kramer and that bunch. They’d call me down on to the field, a bunch of ‘em. I came out unintelligible the football.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah, and he’d tell them what they did that was wrong, and so this one time, he was showing them what they did that was wrong, and he did the same thing, and boy did they…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Taking moving pictures of it, so they couldn’t get out of it Hap laughs.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But when he feels good, and they practice, sometimes they practice twice a day, in spring football practice…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: in April.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And he would walk up there and come home and go back for the afternoon practice, and nearly always got a ride back with somebody, but he always walked up.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Bob Haynes, he drives right by the house, he’s professor there at the unintelligible plant, engineering plant, down in the unintelligible there. So he always comes over to see if I’ve got a ride. I never have to walk home.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But last spring he missed it all.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, last spring I was in the unintelligible hospital for three weeks. Sam: And the team didn’t do too well last year, did they?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Oh Bertha laughs. Let’s not lay that on Hap. Bertha laughs cut Sam: What was it like working for Dr. Robinson?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well, I just typed names on the envelopes and help put out the mail, I didn’t work at the place that they put out the lessons. I mean where they worked them up. But we used to have lots of fun. Sam: Did you know Dr. Robinson? Did you…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah, yeah. Hap was a real good friend of Dr. Robinson.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Yeah, I had a special deputy. He gave me a gold badge for making him the special deputy. Sam: What was he like? How do you remember him?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well I first knew him as just an ordinary clerk in the corner drugstore. He wasn’t anything but just an ordinary clerk in the corner drugstore. And I don’t know just how they got started on this stuff. Hap laughs Oh yeah.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Actually, he was, he did lots of good, he didn’t want his name announced, kids would be lost…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: He’d hire an airplane to search for ‘em.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah, but he didn’t want his name…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: …his name in it all. He always drank his coffee out of a saucer Hap laughs. Yeah, he was pretty generous Hap laughs. Let’s see…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The year that…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: unintelligible on one of these raids, I guess, and that was…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: No, are you gonna tell him about her getting, loaning the money. After Charlie Summerfield died, Hap and I went huckleberrying. And we would go out and pick huckleberries for three or four days, come back, and sell them for a dollar a gallon. And…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: We picked 285 gallons.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: 287 gallons. Sam whistles And the paper said that “assisted by Mrs. Moody” always. That burned me up Bertha laughs. Well, anyway, Hap run for office that fall, and we came in from huckleberrying, and course Hap had gotten his sleeve torn and had these old pants on, and so on. And we went down to the Idaho Inn for, it wasn’t called Idaho Inn then, I guess, but anyway, the paper, they put up all the returns. And Hap had a big safety pin, as I recall, in his sleeve, and Doc said something about it, and Hap stuck out his hand, like he needed some money, and Doc went home and told the missus, and you know she called up the next day. And she wanted to know if Hap really needed money. Hap laughs But we huckleberried, we began at Beales Butte, I guess, and just went clear round, and we picked a few huckleberries on Mount Hood.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Down in Oregon. You see, unintelligible in July, and Democrats put in a man of the name of Berry as sheriff. And Hap laughs so we picked huckleberries and campaigned against Mr. Berry. Hap laughs Guess they should have been smarter than that because at the end of the day they’d have had a Democrat sheriff for six months. Sam: Why, you did, say they should have been smarter. Is that because he didn’t stand much of a chance against you?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well, they should have known that the chief deputy should have been appointed for the rest of that term, you know, six months.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Where the sheriff was a Republican, this is generally what happens, but anyway. Sam: Did Dr. Robinson have a lot of influence in Moscow itself? I’d understood that the religion, that his religion, Psychiana, was kept out of the area.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Never, never, he never…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: That’s right, we couldn’t...

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Never put a letter out in Moscow. Never. Always foreign countries.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: If they was an address, he bought addresses, and if there was an address to anybody here in Moscow, we threw it out. Sam: What was the reason for that? Do you know?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I haven’t any idea.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No. He worked god-forbidden foreign lands, because I know he got several thousand dollars from some unintelligible faith over in India. Yeah, he had the visions you know, he talked to the…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: He talked to mother, wasn’t it his mother, uncle, somebody. But anyway, now one of the things that was in his book was: goldfish jumped out of the goldfish bowl. And he was going to put it back in, he thought well if it could jump out, it could jump back in.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: And it did. Hap laughs

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You know, this is something I don’t know whether anybody that worked for him really knew and understood about.
