Course all I did was, as I say, I typed addresses, and we stuffed, we’d get tired, she’d let us change, and we’d stuff envelopes with these lessons. Sam: Did he seem like a religious person in the same way that the reverends and you know the heads of the churches around did?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, not exactly. He got to be a bishop, you know.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Well, that was his title, yeah. I really couldn’t, I don’t know. Sam: I just wondered how he seemed, that’s all.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Uh huh. He was big guy. I remember one time we was in Portland. He had this huge diamond ring, and we couldn’t see him when we went in this hotel, we couldn’t see him, but the way he was sitting we could see that diamond ring, and Hap said that has to be Doc Robinson. And it was! We liked him. We wasn’t that close, you know, that we knew that much about him. Sam: I think few people did, really.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: Yeah, I think that’s true. Yeah.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: He was generous. He gave away lots of money.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But I guess Alfred didn’t want anything to do with it. And I don’t know whether they have anything left or not, you know, of his lessons and stuff. Sam: I think there’s some records at the university, in the library.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: I hope, I really do. But people would come. One of the girls that lived upstairs, her mother would write and ask about this Psychiana, you know. Well, we didn’t know enough to tell her anything. He had those two buildings that he had workers in. And the first day that I worked Bertha laughs he had a heart attack and they had to take him on the stretcher and Hap said that was some scenery work. Hap laughs Jenny Nesbitt was at the head of the building where I worked, and that was down in the professional building. And then the other was up here. We called it “Sam’s.”

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Used to be Sam’s Furniture. It’s now Dahman, now, I guess, insurance. Weisel, Weisel’s insurance building now.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But I tell you, some of us ladies had a good time. Doc would come in and we’d quiet down Bertha laughs. It got to be, we had I think ten minutes that we could go out, either morning or afternoon, for, get a cup of coffee or whatever we wanted to do. And you know, it gets to be, fifteen, twenty, half an hour, and he watched, I guess, some of us, and so he put a stop to our staying so long. But that’s all right, anybody else would too. pause His daughter and Alf was going to school. And I liked those kids too, but we knew ‘em. Hap knew Doc better than I did. cut Sam: Names of streets and…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: …everything. Let’s see. It started out with Pine. Larch. Cedar.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Oak. “Owns.”

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: “Potlatch Lumber Company Owns.” At that was Oak. “Everything” Elm. Maple was “Most.” Sam: So put together it’s…

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: “Potlatch Lumber Company Owns.” Oh yeah. Sam: “Owns Most Everything.”

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: “Everything Most,” because Maple was last.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Going on up the hill.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: They got some new ones I guess. At one time there was a Fir Street, that was used, temporary buildings down there. So I suppose that was "For Potlatch." Yep. Sam: You said 66 prisoners at one time in the jail? in that little hole in the ground underneath the…this was later?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: No, no.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: The one they tore down, to build this new one.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They built the new jail in 1928. And it was a pretty good-sized jail, and it was course new, and that’s one reason that the federals, qualified to hold federal prisoners.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And that’s the one where they’d board on unintelligible

speaker: Hap Moody
words: There was a hole in the roof.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But things was, they wouldn’t be allowed to keep prisoners now like they did. Then you could put them together.

speaker: Hap Moody
words: They fed them twice a day, I guess now they have to feed ‘em three times.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: But you did take in a lunch if they wanted it?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Hm?

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: You did give ‘em a lunch though, I mean, and sandwich or something if they wanted it later, didn’t you? Sam: How many would you get in a cell, then?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Hm? Sam: How many would you put in a cell?

speaker: Hap Moody
words: Well you see the upstairs, there was two large rooms upstairs, and they had bunks, in big rooms. And there were smaller rooms when they’d get sick. And one room for crazy people. And then we could put them in the basement, that was a full basement under it, six or more down there, and then we had the kitchen and the dining room. unintelligible fill up the whole darn unintelligible if we had to Hap laughs. It was pretty crowded. And we always had a good cook, because all of them bootleggers, there was always a good cook amongst them. And of course, he did the cooking. And the dining room was next to the kitchen. And it wasn’t a very big kitchen, but it had a big stove.

speaker: Bertha Clyde Moody
words: And these old-fashioned electric stoves at the top heated. It would take about an hour to really heat up. But…

speaker: Hap Moody
words: He fed ‘em pancakes, unintelligible nine and four, twice a day. Sam: How long would you keep them? Would you keep the federal prisoners for a pretty long time, too?

speaker: Hap Moody

words: They had these little electric stoves that they just wanted to heat up coffee or something real quick, too. But they didn’t have to wait the hour for the stove to heat up.
